title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 24 — Cao Cao Murders the Consort Dong / Liu Bei Seeks Refuge With Yuan Shao
text:
At the close of the last chapter, followingCao Cao’s discovery of the“girdle” decree, he had convened an assembly of his advisers to consider deposingEmperor Xianin favor of some more worthy successor.

YetCheng Yuargued against this, saying, “It is only because of Your Excellency’s advocacy for the cause of theHouse of Hanthat you are able to exert your authority over the four corners of the land and to issue orders to all the realm. To rashly implement the removal of the Han sovereign in exchange for another, when the lords are not yet pacified, would simply incite armed resistance.”

Cao Caothus abandoned the project, contenting himself with eliminatingDong Chengand his fellows. All five of the conspirators with every member of their households, more than seven hundred all told, were taken and put to death at one or another of the gates of the city. Not one of the officials or people of the city who witnessed these scenes failed to shed tears, and a certain poet in later ages composed a poem lamenting the fate ofDong Cheng:

His purse hides holy writ

To free the sacred words

To save his sovereign ward

He’ll bear this tender trust

Loving until his heart aches

Raging even in his dreams

Devotion is what we remember

Life or death but details

Another poet wrote of the sad fate of Wang Zifu and his friends:

At a stroke they loosed their plot

In a passion they pledged their fortunes

Sheer gall, that cost a hundred lives!

Such zeal secured them a thousand years

Yet even this slaughter of the conspirators and their whole households did not appease the wrath of theAssisting Chancellor, and with his sword buckled at his waist, he went into the palace to slay Noble Consort Dong as well. She was a sister ofDong Chengand, having receivedEmperor Xian’s affections, was by now in the fifth month of pregnancy. At that very momentEmperor Xianwas in the harem, privately meeting with Empress Fu to wonder whyDong Cheng’s actions had not yet borne fruit.Cao Cao’s sudden entrance into the palace, sword at his waist and anger on his face, causedEmperor Xianto turn pale in shock.

“Was Your Majesty aware thatDong Chengwas plotting rebellion?” demandedCao Cao.

“Dong Zhuohas long since been punished,” repliedthe Emperor.

“NotDong Zhuo;Dong Cheng!” roaredCao Cao.

The Emperor’s heart trembled, but he gasped out, “Really, I did not know!”

“And I suppose you have forgotten about cutting your finger and writing out a decree too?”

WhenEmperor Xiancould offer no response,Cao Caobade his lictors seize Consort Dong. At lastEmperor Xianexclaimed, “Consort Dong is in her fifth month, Prime Minister; take pity, I pray!”

“If Heaven had not interposed and defeated the plot I should be a dead man,” saidCao Cao. “How could I leave this woman to work her evil upon me?”

“Then confine her to the Cold Palace until she has given birth to the child,” said Empress Fu. “It will not be too late to kill her then.”

“And have me spare the wicked offspring to avenge the mother?” saidCao Cao.

Consort Dong wept as she said, “Please, let me die with my body intact and don’t expose my corpse.”

Cao Caobade his men show her the white silk cord.The Emperorwept bitterly.

“May you never hate me in the realms below the Nine Springs!” he told her.

And as he spoke, his tears fell like rain, withEmpressFujoining in the lamentations. “Again with these childish sentiments!” fumedCao Cao. He ordered the lictors to hustleConsort Dongout and strangle her in the courtyard. She too received the sympathy of a certain later poet:

In the lover’s bed, a fruitless embrace

In the mother’s bosom, a double death

In all his majesty, powerless to save

The sovereign screens his spring of tears

When leaving the palaceCao Caogave strict orders to the keepers: “From now on, have any imperial in-law who enters the palace without my orders beheaded. Those who are not vigilant in guarding the palace will suffer the same crime with them.”

And to keep an eye on things, he assigned three thousand of his trusted soldiers to theImperial Guardsand appointedCao Hongto command them.

ThenCao Caosaid toCheng Yu, “The conspirators inthe capitalhave been removed, it is true, but there are yet two others,Ma TengandLiu Bei. We can’t fail to get rid of them as well.”

Cheng Yureplied, “AlthoughMa Tenghas his forces camped at Xiliang and could not be taken easily, you could simply write him letters of praise and encouragement in order to allay his suspicions and then entice him to come to the capital, where he would be within your power. Yet not only isLiu Beino less dangerous a foe, arrayed as he is in an ox-horn formation out in Xuzhou,Yuan Shaotoo has troops camped at Guandu and his one desire is to attack Xudu; any sudden campaign towards the east will surely provokeLiu Beiinto seeking aid fromYuan Shao. IfYuan Shaowere to exploit our absence to launch a surprise attack on the capital, what could we do?”

“You have them backwards,” repliedCao Cao. “It’sLiu Beiwho is the real man of action; if we don’t attack him now and wait until he is fully fledged and winged, he will be perilous to deal with.Yuan Shaomay be strong, but he’s so hesitant that he can’t make up his mind. How could he be worth any concern?”

As they were discussing these thingsGuo Jiacame in, andCao Caoreferred the matter to him.

“I aim to campaign east againstLiu Bei, yet there is the matter ofYuan Shao,”Cao Caosaid. “What do you think?”

“Yuan Shaoby nature is dilatory and hesitating, and his various advisers are jealous of one another; he is not to be feared,” saidGuo Jia. “AndLiu Beihas only just put together an army and has not yet won their hearts. Lead your troops east on campaign, Prime Minister, and you could settle things in one battle.”

“Just as I was thinking!” beamedCao Cao. And he raised a great army of two hundred thousand soldiers, to move in five divisions againstXu province.

Scouts took the news of these preparations toXu province.Sun Qianfirst went toXiapito tellLord Guan, and then went toXiapito tellXuande, who reasoned, “We shall have to seek help fromYuan Shaoin order to counteract this threat.” Thus he wrote out a letter and gave it toSun Qianto bring north of the Yellow River to visitYuan Shao’s domain.Sun Qianfirst sought outTian Feng, and when he had explained the circumstances and had asked him to lead troops into the field,Tian Fengpromptly broughtSun Qianin for an audience withYuan Shaoto present the letter.

ButYuan Shaowas of melancholy countenance, and his dress was all awry.

Tian Fengsaid, “Why this disarray, my lord?”

“I am about to die,” repliedYuan Shao.

“Why do you say such a thing?” askedTian Feng.

“I have five sons, but onlythe youngestis clever enough to understand my thoughts. Now he is suffering fromscabies, and his life is about to end,” bemoanedYuan Shao. “How can I have any heart to talk over such affairs?”

“But,” saidTian Feng, “the present combination of circumstances is unparalleled.Cao Caois going to attack the east andXuchangwill be empty. You can enter it with a few volunteers, and so perform good service tothe Emperor, and save the people from sorrow. You have only but to act.”

“I know the chance is excellent, but I am worried and distressed and fear failure.”

“What are you distressed about?” askedTian Feng.

“Among my sons only this special one is remarkable; and if anything were to happen to him, I am done.”

Thus it became evident that no army would be dispatched.

In confirmation of this,Yuan Shaosaid toSun Qian, “Go home and tellXuandethe real reason for my inaction, and say that if anything untoward happens, he can come over to me, and I will find some means of helping him.”

Tian Fengstruck the ground with his staff. “It is such a pity!” cried he. “Just as a unique opportunity presents itself, everything is spoiled by the illness of a child!” And he left.

Sun Qiansaw that no help could be hoped for and set out to return. When he had arrived and related what he had seen,Xuandewas alarmed, and asked what could be done.

“Do not be troubled, brother,” saidZhang Fei. “We can destroyCao Caowith a sudden attack before his army has had time to make camp!”

“That would be according to the rules of war,” saidXuande. “You have always been a bold warrior, and that move againstLiu Daishows that you are becoming a strategist too.”

SoLiu BeigaveZhang Feicommand of enough soldiers to carry out his plan.

Now whileCao Caowas in the midst of his march towardXiapi, a tornado sprang up and the howling gale tore down one of his banners and broke the staff.Cao Caocalled together his leaders and advisers to ask this portended.

Xun Yuasked, “From what direction was the wind, at what was the color of the flag?”

“The wind was from the southeast, and the flag blue and red,” saidCao Cao.

“There is but one interpretation: A raid on the camp occurs tonight,” saidXun Yu.

Cao Caonodded. At that momentMao Jieentered and reported a similar incident. Cao asked him what he thought of the portent.

“My thinking tells me it means a nighttime raid,” repliedMao Jie.

Alas for the weakness of this descendant of kings!

He placed his faith on a night raid,

But the broken staff of a banner warned his enemy.

Why should the ancient of days favor the wicked?

“This is evidently providence,” saidCao Cao.

And so he began to make preparations. He dispatched nine bodies of men to take stations, leaving only one camped. The others he placed in ambush at eight points.

There was but little moonlight asXuandeandZhang Feimarched their respective armies towardCao Cao’s camp. They had leftSun Qianto guardXiapi.Zhang Fei, since he was the originator of the stratagem, led the way with some light horse. As they drew near everything seemed very quiet and no one seemed moving. Then suddenly lights flashed out all about them andZhang Feisaw he had fallen into a trap.

At once the ambushing troops sprang forth from all eight directions. To the east,Zhang Liao; and to the westXu Chu. To the south,Yu Jin; and to the north,Li Dian. To the southeast,Xu Huang; and to the southwest,Yue Jin. To the northeast,Xiahou Dun; and to the northwest,Xiahou Yuan.

Zhang Fei, dashing this way and rushing that, guarding his vanguard while protecting his rear, vainly attempting to clear himself. The soldiers he had, being originallyCao Cao’s men, soon gave in and defected to their old leader. His position grewdesperate.1•••

Mao: these were former soldiers ofZhu Ling,Lu Zhao, andChe Zhou.↩

Zhang FeimetXu Huangand engaged, butYu Jinpressed him from behind. At length he slashed a bloody path free from the confrontation, and with only a few dozen riders started back toXiaopei. But his path was blocked. He thought to make forXiapiorXu province, but felt certain that either path would also be barred. No other way seemed open and so he made for theMangdang Hills.

AsXuandedrew near the camp he intended to attack he heard the din of battle. Then he was attacked in the rear and very soon had lost half his force. NextXiahou Duncame to attack.Xuandebolted, and was pursued byXiahou Yuan. PresentlyLiu Beilooked about him and found less than half a hundred soldiers remained in his following. He set his face in the direction ofXiaopei.

But before long he saw that place was in flames. So he changed his plan and went towardXiapi. However he found the whole countryside full of the enemy and he could not get through. So he bethought himself of the promise ofYuan Shao, that he would find refuge if things went agley, and determined to go to him until he could form some other plan. Wherefore he took theQing provinceroad, but it was also blocked. And so he went into the open country and made his way north, not without being pursued and losing the remainder of his few followers.

He hastened towardQingzhou City, traveling three hundred li a day. When he reached the city and summoned the gate the guards asked who he was and they told the governor, who wasYuan Shao’s eldest son,Yuan Tan.Yuan Tanwas greatly surprised, but he opened the gates and went to meetXuande, whom he treated with due consideration.

Liu Beitold the story of his defeat and said he wished for harbor. He was given suitable quarters and hospitably entertained, while the young man wrote to inform his father.Yuan Tanthen provided an escort and sentXuandeon his journey to the boundary ofPingyuan.

AtYejunhe was met byYuan Shaoin person, with a great escort.Xuandemade a humble obeisance whichYuan Shaohastened to return, and said, “I have been very distressed that, on account of my son’s illness, I did not come to your aid. It is great joy to see you; the one desire of my life is satisfied.”

Xuandereplied, “The poorLiu Beiyou see before you has long desired to take refuge with you, fate has hitherto denied him that privilege. Now, attacked byCao Cao, my family lost, I remembered that you, General, would receive good people from all sides. Wherefore I put my pride in my pocket. I trust that I may be found worthy and one day I will prove my gratitude.”

Yuan Shaoreceived him with great pleasure and treated him well, and they lived together inJi province.

After the capture ofXiapi,Cao Caopressed on towardXu province, which, after a short defense, and the flight ofMi ZhuandJian Yong, was surrendered byChen Deng.Cao Caoled his army into the city, restored order, and pacified the people. Next he wanted to press on toXiapi, whereGuan Yuwas holding out and guardingLiu Bei’s family.

Xun Yusaid, “Guan Yuis there, in charge of his brother’s family, and he will defend the city to the last. If you do not strike quickly,Yuan Shaowill!”2•••

Mao: “Xun Wenruounderstood thatXuandehad taken refuge withYuan Shao, and thatYuan Shaowould would not remain complacent for long.” Events in this chapter took place in the spring ofAD 200.↩

“I have always lovedGuan Yu, both for his warlike abilities and his principles. I would entreat him to enter my service. Could anyone talk him into surrender?”

“He will not do that,” saidGuo Jia. “His sense of loyalty is too deep. I fear anyone sent to speak with him would lose his life.”

ButZhang Liaostepped forward suddenly, saying, “I am acquainted with him, and would like to attempt it.”

Cheng Yulooked at him and said, “Though you are an old acquaintance, I do not think you are equal to talkingGuan Yuover to our side. But I have a scheme that will so entangle him that he will have no alternative. He will have to enter the service oftheAssisting Chancellor.”

They set the fatal spring beside the lordly tiger’s trail,

They hide the hook with fragrant bait to catch the mighty whale.

How wasGuan Yuto be entrapped? It will be told in the next chapter.
